title: Give the board a voice
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire an active buzzer through an NPN transistor, add a push button, upload the Arduino sketch, and turn your board into a doorbell — press for a beep, let go for silence. While you build it, learn what that beep actually is: sound is air being pushed back and forth, and its pitch is a frequency you can set. That is the idea the passive-buzzer siren pays off, and the counterpart to Day 7, where the pin's on-time set brightness.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the Buzzer: an active buzzer driven from GPIO 14 through an NPN transistor with a 1 kilo-ohm resistor on its base and the buzzer powered from the 5V rail, plus a push button on GPIO 21 held by 10 kilo-ohm resistors, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: Active buzzer
parts.explanation: A buzzer turns electricity into sound. An active buzzer has its own oscillator inside, so switching it on plays one fixed tone. A passive buzzer has no oscillator — you feed it a changing signal to choose the pitch, which is how you get melodies or a siren.

parts.name: NPN transistor
parts.explanation: A GPIO pin can switch things but only supplies a trickle of current — not enough for a buzzer. An NPN transistor uses that trickle on its base to switch a much larger current from the 5V rail through the buzzer. It is an electrically controlled switch.

parts.name: Push button
parts.explanation: This push button has four pins in two connected pairs. Pressing it bridges the pairs and completes the circuit; releasing it breaks the connection again. It is the simplest way to hand the board a yes-or-no input.

parts.name: 1 kΩ resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor limits how much current flows. Placed in series with the LED, the 220 Ω one keeps the current gentle enough to light the LED instead of burning it out. The bands of colour print its value, and it has no direction.

parts.name: 2 × 10 kΩ resistors
parts.explanation: An input pin left floating drifts between HIGH and LOW and reads at random. A 10 kΩ resistor gently ties the pin to a known level, so it reads a steady value until a button press forces the other one.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: The buzzer draws more current than a pin can give so it runs off 5V.
The pin switches the buzzer through the transistor.
The board reads this pin to catch the press.
Holds GPIO 21 HIGH until a press pulls it LOW.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Land the core idea- sound is vibrating air, and pitch is frequency, the number of on/off cycles a second that reach the buzzer.
Tie it to Day 7- there the duty cycle set brightness; here the frequency sets pitch while the duty stays roughly fixed, the same fast-switching pin doing a new job.
Explain active versus passive plainly- an active buzzer has its own oscillator and plays one fixed note, while a passive buzzer is silent until the pin feeds it a frequency, which tone() generates.
Point out that musical notes are just specific frequencies, so 262 Hz is a middle C and 392 Hz the G above it.
Check the transistor's orientation first — emitter, base, and collector each have a job, and a reversed transistor is the most common cause of no sound.
Confirm the buzzer's + pin goes to the 5V rail, not 3.3V, or the sound will be faint or absent.
If the buzzer always sounds, suspect the button wiring or its 10 kΩ pull-up on GPIO 21.
Keep the Arduino path primary; the passive-buzzer pitch experiment and MicroPython are optional only if already set up.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the transistor on the breadboard and note its three legs — emitter, base, and collector.
Wire the buzzer's + pin to the 5V rail and its other pin down to the transistor.
Connect the transistor's base to GPIO 14 through the 1 kΩ resistor.
Wire the push button so one side reaches GPIO 21, then add its 10 kΩ pull-up to 3.3V.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_06.1_Doorbell.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.

arduino.explanation: True only while the button is held — a press pulls GPIO 21 LOW.
Sends the pin HIGH, which switches the buzzer on through the transistor.
The passive-buzzer variant (Sketch_06.2_Alertor) uses ledcWriteTone to choose the pitch and sweep a siren.

micropython.explanation: Reads GPIO 21 as an input with a built-in pull-up — the same job the 10 kΩ resistor does in hardware.
Drives GPIO 14, which switches the buzzer through the transistor.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Sound is air vibrating, and pitch is how many on/off cycles a second reach the buzzer — the counterpart to Day 7, where the pin's on-time set brightness.
theoryModel.formula: on/off cycles per second = frequency = pitch (Day 7 varied on-time for brightness; here the rate sets the note)

notes.title: The tie back to Day 7
notes.body: Day 7's pin also flicked far too fast to watch, and its duty cycle, the share of each cycle held on, set the brightness. Here the duty stays near half and the frequency is the knob, so the same fast-switching pin makes pitch instead of light.

notes.title: Why the active buzzer ignores all this
notes.body: Its built-in oscillator fixes its own pitch, so switching it faster from the pin just chops the same note on and off. Only a passive buzzer hands you the frequency, which is why melodies and sirens need the passive kind.

notes.title: Where the transistor fits
notes.body: The pin still can't supply the buzzer's current, so it switches a transistor and the transistor drives the 5V buzzer. The pin decides on or off; the transistor does the lifting.

expectedOutputExample: Press and hold the button — a steady beep sounds at once.
Every press sounds the same note, for as long as the board has power.
test.successCriteria: An active buzzer plays one fixed tone — choosing the pitch is the passive-buzzer variant's job.

firstChecks: Check the transistor legs are the right way round, and that the buzzer + goes to 5V.
The button or its 10 kΩ pull-up is likely miswired on GPIO 21.
The buzzer may be on 3.3V instead of 5V — move it to the 5V rail.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Play three named frequencies on the passive buzzer, hear each land as a higher note, then string them into a short rising phrase — and confirm the active buzzer can't do it.
